governing_equations: Equilibrium at N = K; inflection near N = K/2 for logistic growth

socratic_plan.question: Looking at the early generations, what do you think will happen to the population as it approaches K? Will it keep accelerating, slow down, or something else?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student predicts the decelerating growth before observing it
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student makes a prediction about the curve shape near K

socratic_plan.question: Click on the point in the simulation where you think the population is growing the fastest.
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student identifies the inflection point as the location of maximum growth rate
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student clicks near the inflection point region

socratic_plan.question: What happens to the shape of the curve if you increase the growth rate r? Try it and describe what you see.
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student discovers that r controls the steepness but not the final level
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student notices the curve rises faster but still levels off at K

socratic_plan.question: The population always levels off at K regardless of the growth rate. What do you think K represents in a real ecosystem?
socratic_plan.learning_goal: Student connects the mathematical ceiling to the real-world concept of carrying capacity
socratic_plan.exit_condition: Student articulates that K is set by resource limits in the environment

annotate.text: Growth rate peaks here at K/2

verification.summary: Higher intrinsic growth rate r reaches the near-capacity band in fewer generations while converging to the same K; final reported population should stay near K across r for fixed K and n0.

probes.description: Low r — slower approach to carrying capacity
Higher r (still moderate) so discrete steps reach the near-capacity band in the verifier window.

invariants.description: Generation index when crossing the near-capacity band should decrease as r increases (fixed K, n0).
Final population at the near-capacity event should match across growth rates for the same K.
